title: Princeton/IAS Symplectic Geometry Seminar
speaker: Keon Choi
abstract: Embedded contact homology is an invariant of a contact three-manifold, which is recently shown to be isomorphic to Heegaard Floer homology and Seiberg-Witten Floer homology. However, ECH chain complex depends on the contact form on the manifold and the almost complex structure on its symplectization. This fact can be used to extract symplectic geometric information (e.g. ECH capacities) but explicit computation of the chain complexes has been carried out only on a few cases.

proof_stages.label: Motivation for direct ECH chain computations
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates computing the ECH chain complex directly, rather than only via its isomorphisms with Heegaard Floer and Seiberg-Witten Floer homology, because the chain-level theory depends on the contact form and encodes quantitative contact/symplectic information.

proof_stages.label: ECH chain complex and index-one curve differential
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the ECH setup for a nondegenerate contact three-manifold: admissible orbit sets generate the chain complex, and the differential counts ECH-index-one holomorphic curves in the symplectization.

proof_stages.label: Hutchings-Sullivan polygonal rounding rule for the convex T3 model
proof_stages.summary: For the standard T3 contact form treated by Hutchings and Sullivan, Morse-Bott Reeb families are perturbed to elliptic and hyperbolic orbits, orbit sets are encoded as ordered polygonal paths, and the differential is described by rounding a two-edge corner with a specified labeling rule.

proof_stages.label: Convex and concave orbit types for general T2-invariant forms
proof_stages.summary: For a general T2-invariant contact form on an interval times T2, the graph of the coefficient functions determines whether the Reeb vector field rotates counterclockwise or clockwise, leading after perturbation to convex E-check/H-check and concave E-hat/H-hat orbit types with corresponding Conley-Zehnder indices.

proof_stages.label: Polygonal-region formalism for hybrid contact forms
proof_stages.summary: To handle contact forms with both convex and concave portions, the talk defines polygonal regions between alpha and beta paths with x-coordinate data, extreme and local edges, decomposability, slice classes, positivity with respect to the Reeb vector field, and minimality.

proof_stages.label: General combinatorial criterion for nonzero differentials
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that, after a suitable perturbation, a differential coefficient is nonzero exactly when the differing middle region between two orbit sets is positive, nonlocal, indecomposable, and minimal, with all edge labels following the extreme/nonextreme convex-concave rule except for exactly one allowed elliptic-hyperbolic swap.

examples.name: ECH capacities in symplectic embedding problems
examples.note: ECH capacities are mentioned as an application showing that contact-form-dependent chain-level ECH data can carry quantitative symplectic information lost under the abstract Floer-homology isomorphisms.
examples.engagement_reason: ECH capacities are cited as useful for symplectic embedding problems, but no capacity or embedding computation is carried out.
